fine_dining.name: Le Chat-Botté
signature_dishes: Léman féra carpaccio with caviar and lemon confit
fine_dining.atmosphere: Refined lakefront dining room inside the Beau-Rivage hotel, with Mont Blanc views and white-glove service
fine_dining.best_time: Dinner, especially for the seasonal tasting menu
fine_dining.booking_tips: Reserve 2-3 weeks ahead; request a window table for the Mont Blanc view; jacket required for men at dinner
fine_dining.local_tips: Lunch service offers a more affordable entry point to the cooking. Pair with a Lavaux Chasselas from the deep cellar.

fine_dining.name: Bayview by Michel Roth
signature_dishes: Tarte au chocolat noir grand cru
fine_dining.atmosphere: Sleek glass-walled dining room on top of the Hotel President with the city's most expansive lake panorama
fine_dining.booking_tips: Book the chef's tasting menu for the kitchen view; request bay-facing window
fine_dining.local_tips: Roth's wine pairings lean heavily on Geneva-region producers, including small AOC Chasselas growers worth exploring.

fine_dining.name: Tsé Fung
signature_dishes: Peking duck served in two services
Steamed dim sum trolley at lunch
fine_dining.atmosphere: Dramatic Chinese-modern room inside La Réserve hotel with red lacquer accents and lake-garden access
fine_dining.best_time: Lunch dim sum or weekend Peking duck dinner
fine_dining.booking_tips: Book Peking duck 24 hours ahead; dim sum lunch is the best-value Michelin meal in the city
fine_dining.location: La Réserve, Route de Lausanne 301 (15 min from centre)
fine_dining.local_tips: The only Cantonese restaurant in Switzerland with a Michelin star — worth the cab ride.

fine_dining.name: Domaine de Châteauvieux
signature_dishes: Whole roast Bresse chicken for two
fine_dining.atmosphere: 16th-century farmhouse in vineyards 20 minutes from the city, with terrace overlooking the Rhône valley
fine_dining.best_time: Long weekend lunch in spring or autumn
fine_dining.booking_tips: Book the tasting menu and request an outdoor table; reserve at least a month ahead in autumn (game season)
fine_dining.local_tips: Geneva's most celebrated rural-fine-dining experience; Philippe Chevrier has held two stars for over two decades.

casual_dining.name: Café du Soleil
signature_dishes: Filets de perche meunière with frites
casual_dining.atmosphere: 1834 institution in Petit-Saconnex — beam ceilings, white tablecloths, packed local crowd
casual_dining.booking_tips: Reserve a table by the window or in the back room; can be booked online via the website
casual_dining.local_tips: Genevans will argue endlessly about the city's best fondue, but Café du Soleil is on every shortlist for a reason.

casual_dining.name: Les Armures
signature_dishes: Roesti with cheese and fried egg
casual_dining.atmosphere: Old Town stalwart with armoured-knight decor and heavy wood beams; every visiting head of state since the 1980s has eaten here
casual_dining.booking_tips: Reserve a back-room booth for atmosphere; closed Sundays in low season
casual_dining.local_tips: Touristy but consistently excellent fondue. Ask for the kirsch-flambé version if you want the full ceremony.

casual_dining.name: Café du Centre
signature_dishes: Filets de perche from Lake Geneva
casual_dining.atmosphere: Belle Époque brasserie with a vast central island bar, dating to 1871 — the city's oldest continuous brasserie
casual_dining.best_time: Long lunch on Place du Molard
casual_dining.booking_tips: Walk-in friendly at lunch; reserve dinner Thursday–Saturday
casual_dining.local_tips: The terrace on Place du Molard is one of the best people-watching spots in central Geneva.

casual_dining.name: Le Relais de l'Entrecôte
signature_dishes: Sliced entrecôte with green herb sauce and frites — single dish, two services
casual_dining.atmosphere: Classic Parisian-style bistro with no menu choice and queues out the door
casual_dining.best_time: Early dinner (6:30pm) to avoid the worst queues
casual_dining.booking_tips: Walk-ins only; queues from 7:30pm onwards; weekday lunch is fastest
casual_dining.local_tips: Same green-sauce formula as the Paris original. Save room for the profiteroles.

casual_dining.name: Café des Négociants
casual_dining.atmosphere: Buzzy bistro in Carouge with a vine-shaded terrace; the strongest natural-wine list in town
casual_dining.booking_tips: Book a terrace table for warm-weather lunches; closed Sundays
casual_dining.local_tips: Order the dish of the day chalked above the bar — usually offal or seasonal game from Geneva-region farms.

street_food.name: Manakish & shawarma on Rue de Berne (Pâquis)
street_food.atmosphere: Stand-up counters and small storefronts, open into the small hours
street_food.booking_tips: No bookings — just walk in. Parfums de Beyrouth and Boulangerie du Levant are reliably excellent
street_food.location: Rue de Berne and Rue de Neuchâtel, Pâquis
street_food.local_tips: Geneva's cheapest sit-down dinner is a manakish and labneh combo for under CHF 12.

street_food.name: Plainpalais Wednesday Food Trucks
street_food.atmosphere: Open-air park-side market with 15+ rotating trucks
street_food.local_tips: Cash is still useful at some trucks. Many do happy-hour specials Friday evenings.

coffee_shops.name: Boréal Coffee Shop
coffee_shops.atmosphere: Minimalist Scandinavian-style cafés, the closest thing Geneva has to a Melbourne or Oslo coffee scene
coffee_shops.location: Multiple branches: Old Town (Rue Verdaine), Pâquis, Eaux-Vives, Cornavin
coffee_shops.local_tips: The Rue Verdaine flagship has the most extensive single-origin offering.

coffee_shops.name: Bains des Pâquis Kiosk
coffee_shops.atmosphere: Communal pier-side counter open every day of the year — locals' default morning stop
coffee_shops.location: Bains des Pâquis pier, Quai du Mont-Blanc
coffee_shops.local_tips: Run by a cooperative — profits fund the pier and sauna. Order at the counter, take outside.

coffee_shops.name: Café Remor
coffee_shops.atmosphere: Belle Époque corner café with a marble bar and the city's longest-running pâtisserie counter
coffee_shops.local_tips: Their truffes au chocolat and merveilles (donut puffs) are the platonic ideal of a Genevan tea-time.

bars.name: Atelier Cocktail Club
signature_drinks: Negroni variations on a barrel-aged tap
bars.atmosphere: Dimly lit, jazz-soundtracked back room in the Old Town with a serious bar program
bars.local_tips: Sit at the bar to watch the team work. Frequently ranked in Switzerland's top 5 cocktail bars.

bars.name: Café des Banques
signature_drinks: Local Chasselas wines by the glass
bars.atmosphere: Standing-room corner spot in the banking district that fills with Geneva's finance crowd from 6pm
bars.local_tips: Free pretzels and saucisson with apéro orders — Geneva tradition.

bars.name: Yvette de Marseille
bars.atmosphere: Eccentric Marseille-tinged dive with mismatched chairs and Côte d'Azur kitsch; beloved late-night spot
bars.local_tips: Open until 2am most nights. Ask the bartender for an unlisted pastis flight.

food_tours.name: Geneva Food & Walking Tour
highlights: Chocolatier stop at Auer or Stettler
Old Town wine bar visit with Chasselas pairing
Stop at a charcuterie for longeole sausage
food_tours.location: Meets in front of the cathedral
food_tours.local_tips: Run by Edible Geneva and Geneva Tour — both excellent. Book a week ahead, smaller groups (max 8).

food_tours.name: Carouge Market & Bistro Tour
highlights: Producer introductions at Place du Marché
Cheese tasting with an AOC fromager
food_tours.local_tips: Better tour than the central Geneva equivalents — smaller, friendlier, more local.

cooking_classes.name: École Hôtelière de Genève — Public Cooking Workshops
cooking_classes.location: Avenue de la Paix 12, near the UN
cooking_classes.local_tips: Genuinely high-level instruction — most participants are aspiring chefs and serious home cooks.

cooking_classes.name: Stettler Chocolate Workshop
cooking_classes.local_tips: Book the December classes early — Stettler's chocolate sells out before Christmas.

markets.name: Plainpalais Market (Marché de Plainpalais)
markets.type: Twice-weekly farmers' market + Sunday flea market
highlights: Local cheeses including Gruyère AOP and Tomme Vaudoise
Sunday flea market (separate stalls, vintage and antiques)
markets.best_time: Saturday morning for the freshest selection
markets.local_tips: Brunch nearby at Brasserie des Halles de l'Île after the market round.

markets.name: Carouge Saturday Market
highlights: Bistros open early for market-day coffee
markets.local_tips: Pair with a long lunch at a Carouge bistro — Café des Négociants or Olivier de Provence.

markets.name: Marché Pâquis (Place de la Navigation)
highlights: Daily fruit and veg, Tuesday to Saturday
Multicultural stalls reflecting the Pâquis demographic
markets.best_time: Morning for produce, late afternoon for prepared foods
markets.local_tips: Geneva's most multicultural market — Lebanese, Sri Lankan and Iberian producers all represented.

spring: Wild garlic (ail des ours) used in soups, butters and pestos
Fresh féra and omble chevalier from Lake Geneva (May reopening of fishing season)
Easter lamb in Old Town bistros
Cardons genevois — Geneva's protected thistle vegetable, in spring re-emergence
summer: Filets de perche from Lake Geneva, served lakeside
Geneva-region Chasselas wines from the new vintage
Fresh strawberries and stone fruit from Satigny and Russin
Cold soups and salads on outdoor terraces
Pavé glacé chocolates eaten alongside lake swims
autumn: Chasse (game) season — wild boar, venison, hare in Old Town bistros
Cardons genevois with gratin sauce, served at Christmas markets in their full season
Wild mushrooms (cèpes, chanterelles) from the Jura
New wine releases at Caves Ouvertes weekends
Pumpkin and chestnut dishes in casual bistros
winter: Raclette with potatoes and pickled onions
Vacherin Mont d'Or — the runny seasonal AOC cheese
Marmite de l'Escalade — chocolate cauldrons filled with marzipan vegetables (December)
Hot vin chaud (mulled wine) at the Old Town Christmas market
